skill.name: bmad-orchestrator
skill.description: Orchestrates BMAD workflows for structured AI-driven development across analysis, planning, solutioning, and implementation phases.
skill.summary: Routes BMAD projects through structured planning, phase gates, status checks, and implementation handoffs.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns. Most markdown command examples and weak-crypto detections are false positives, but executable scripts confirm high-risk installer and command-construction behavior.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The installer downloads https://plannotator.ai/install.sh and immediately executes it with sh. This is a true positive because remote code runs without pinning, checksum verification, or review.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present in executable setup code and documentation. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, but the supply-chain execution risk is concrete.

high_findings.title: Inline Python Injection Risk
high_findings.description: phase-gate-review.sh embeds DOC_FILE and TITLE directly inside a Python command string. Crafted quotes in arguments could alter Python source if the fallback path runs.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are interpolated into quoted Python literals instead of passed as argv or environment variables. Exploitability depends on reaching the fallback path.

medium_findings.title: Local Script Execution and Permission Changes
medium_findings.description: The installer executes a sibling hook script and chmods bundled scripts. This appears intended for setup, but it grants the skill broad local execution impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit and operationally legitimate, but it executes local shell code and changes permissions. Users should review scripts before running them.

medium_findings.title: Project File Creation Without Path Confinement
medium_findings.description: init-project.sh creates directories and writes BMAD files based on user-provided output settings. This is useful setup behavior but lacks explicit path confinement.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The writes are expected for project initialization, and arguments are user-supplied. Risk is moderate because absolute or traversal-like output paths are not rejected.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are examples in documentation rather than hidden execution. They still guide users to run shell commands, so review is advised.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are markdown instructions or command references, not executable code paths. They are safe as text but can influence user behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto hits occur on BMAD labels, markdown references, or hash wording. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, or custom cryptography implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I reviewed representative flagged lines and found workflow text, filenames, and documentation. No cryptographic API or security-sensitive hashing was present.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Searches for override, ignore-instruction, preapproval, and authority phrases found no prompt-injection attempt. One ordinary phrase refers to a reviewed document hash.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only relevant match was normal workflow wording about whether a document had already been reviewed. No evidence found of instructions to bypass this audit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Remote install content is executed through sh without integrity verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl-to-sh pattern is present in executable installer code and setup documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Input Embedded in Inline Python
dangerous_patterns.description: Shell variables are inserted into Python source code instead of being passed as separate arguments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fallback Python snippet directly includes DOC_FILE and TITLE in string literals. This is a clear unsafe construction pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Modification During Setup
dangerous_patterns.description: Setup scripts create project folders, write config files, and adjust executable permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The operations are expected for setup but materially change the local workspace. Risk depends on user intent and execution context.

content.value_statement: AI-assisted projects often lose context between planning and implementation. This skill guides BMAD phases, status checks, and review gates so teams can move work forward in order.
actual_capabilities: Initializes BMAD project folders, config files, and workflow status files.
Checks current BMAD phase status and recommends the next workflow command.
Documents Analysis, Planning, Solutioning, and Implementation phase order.
Submits phase documents to plannotator for review when the CLI is installed.
Provides templates for BMAD project configuration and workflow tracking.
Includes setup guidance for Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, and OpenCode usage.
limitations: The setup flow depends on shell scripts and local command-line tools.
plannotator review requires a separate CLI and may open an external browser UI.
Automatic YAML updates are stronger when yq is installed.
The skill guides workflow structure but does not replace human approval of phase documents.

use_cases.title: Start a structured AI project
use_cases.description: Initialize BMAD files and choose the correct first phase for a new application or feature.

use_cases.title: Control planning handoffs
use_cases.description: Use status checks and phase gates to keep requirements, architecture, and sprint planning aligned.

use_cases.title: Review phase deliverables
use_cases.description: Send PRDs, tech specs, and architecture documents through plannotator before implementation starts.

prompt_templates.title: Check My BMAD Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Use bmad-orchestrator to check the current BMAD workflow status and tell me the next recommended step.

prompt_templates.title: Initialize a New Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Initialize BMAD for a Level 2 web app named Atlas and explain which planning documents should come first.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Phase Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PRD with the BMAD phase gate process and identify what must be approved before architecture begins.

prompt_templates.title: Recover a Complex Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my BMAD status, infer blocked phases, recommend the next two workflows, and note any missing deliverables.

output_examples.input: Check the workflow status for my initialized project.
output: Required item: Product Requirements Document is not started.
Recommended next step: run the PRD workflow before architecture.

output_examples.input: Initialize BMAD for a Level 1 API project.
output: Project configuration is created with Level 1 settings.
Tech spec is marked required, while PRD is recommended.
Story tracking is prepared under the selected documentation folder.

output_examples.input: Gate this architecture document before implementation.
output: The architecture document is prepared for plannotator review.
Implementation should wait until the review result is approved.
The workflow status can be updated after approval.

best_practices: Run setup scripts in dry-run mode before allowing local changes.
Keep BMAD status files committed so phase decisions are reviewable.
Review every phase document before advancing to the next phase.
anti_patterns: Do not skip phase gates when requirements or architecture changed.
Do not run remote installer scripts without reviewing the source first.
Do not treat generated BMAD plans as approved without stakeholder review.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It coordinates BMAD workflows so AI-assisted development follows a clear phase sequence.

faq.question: Does it generate application code?
faq.answer: It focuses on planning, routing, status checks, and phase handoffs. Implementation happens through separate BMAD workflows.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. The documentation also mentions Gemini CLI and OpenCode integration paths.

faq.question: Is plannotator required?
faq.answer: No. The scripts can skip plannotator, but visual phase review depends on the plannotator CLI.

faq.question: Where does it write files?
faq.answer: It creates BMAD configuration, workflow status, command, and story folders in the current project.

faq.question: Should I run the installer directly?
faq.answer: Review the shell scripts first. The installer can fetch and run a remote script from plannotator.ai.
